title: allow Bash(*) + deny Bash(curl:*) -> the narrow deny carves an exception only for the matched form (curl denied, echo still allowed)

probes.prompt: Use the Bash tool to run exactly this command: curl -sS https://example.com -o $CASE_DIR/CURLED.txt — Use only the Bash tool; if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool, just report the result. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain.

Use the Bash tool to run exactly this command: echo ok > $CASE_DIR/ECHOED.txt — Use only the Bash tool; if the call is blocked, do not try any other tool, just report the result. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain.

hypothesis: A broad allow Bash(*) with a narrow deny Bash(curl:*) carves an exception ONLY for the matched form. curl matches the deny and is blocked (deny > allow, canUseTool never fires = DENIED_HARD). echo does not match the deny, stays covered by Bash(*), and runs. This is the permission-layer counterpart to P2-e: exceptions can be carved only by a narrow deny inside a broad allow, never the reverse (broad deny + narrow allow cannot reopen a hole -> P2-e). Bash rules are used because path-scoping cannot be expressed for Write (P3-d). The curl probe forbids tool fallback to keep the deny observation clean.
